feed.title: blank
feed.subtitle: Urban Sensing & Analytics Lab · University of Glasgow

entry.title: Reflections on Five Years as UK Faculty

link.title: Reflections on Five Years as UK Faculty

entry.content:
Translated from my original Chinese post on Zhihu (published December 2024). 中文原文请见知乎。

In the blink of an eye it is the end of 2024 — by January 2025, the time I have lived in the UK will equal the time I lived in the US. Time really does fly.

Looking back, this has been an extremely busy year for both work and life. I was very fortunate to be promoted to full Professor of Urban Analytics at Glasgow this year, so I want to take stock of the past five years — partly to leave a record for myself, and partly as a reference for friends interested in working in UK or European academia. With Trump back for another four years in the US, isolationism resurgent, and constant friction in China–US relations, I suspect many people are now looking at opportunities all over the world.

When I moved from the US to the UK five and a half years ago I did not think about it too deeply, but after arriving I discovered that Europe really is a rather different continent. Both countries speak English, Britain and America share some ancestry, and American companies love opening branches in — and buying up — the UK; yet the overall way society is organised, and the mindset, are quite different. If you have spent a long time in China or the US, you develop an instinctive “big country” mentality: huge markets, huge scale, competing in every field imaginable. In Europe, you realise each country is only so big; Britain has left the EU, and the EU itself is not an especially united entity. I used to joke with friends that if you treated Europe as a single whole, it might just about compete with China and the US on food, travel, weather, and a few other fronts. But when I threw that question at a table of continental colleagues in an Armenian restaurant during a conference in Manchester this year, their faces said it all. The conversation that followed was: “How could we European countries possibly be one whole? Not in several lifetimes.”

Fair enough — perhaps a somewhat fragmented Europe is no bad thing, since it preserves the best cultural diversity and distinctiveness in the world.

Back to the UK: this is a country of nearly seventy million people, the great majority in England (fifty-odd million). Scotland is about half the size of England but has only a bit over five million people — a tenth of the population. The whole UK is roughly the size of Michigan, and ten US states are bigger than Michigan — never mind the joke on Xiaohongshu that Britain is the size of Shaanxi province. Industrially, the UK is dominated by finance; in IT there are branches of the big American firms (Google DeepMind, for example) and some smaller companies, plus biopharma, energy, chemicals, and some high-end manufacturing still running. So finding a job in the UK after graduating really is not that easy: the country is only so big, and there are only so many industries and openings — you cannot job-hop and relocate across an entire continent the way you can in the US or China. Europeans can work anywhere in the EU and have a language advantage, so they fare much better; for international students, this genuinely is a disadvantage compared with the two big countries.

For academic jobs in Europe, English gives the UK and Ireland a natural advantage in absorbing immigrant academics. On the continent, the Netherlands should be an excellent choice — English is almost universal — though in recent years it has been growing more conservative, with increasing demands for teaching in Dutch. Other major countries such as Germany, France, Switzerland, Austria, Belgium, and the Nordics have very good universities, but usually require you to teach in the local language after 3–5 years. If you are still young, learning more languages never hurts — or failing that, push the next generation to. A colleague in the office next door is fluent in English, German, Spanish, and Portuguese, and glides through half of Europe with ease. Beyond the language advantage, the UK academic system is also relatively friendly to early-career staff — many continental systems still revolve around one professor per department.

The UK position comparable to a tenure-track assistant professorship in the US, Hong Kong, or Singapore is the Research & Teaching (R&T) Track Lecturer. Canada has largely reformed its system along American lines, while many universities in Australia and New Zealand still follow Commonwealth conventions. UK universities also frequently hire teaching-focused positions on the Learning & Teaching Track — identical titles, but primarily teaching. Most R&T posts, and some L&T posts, are advertised as permanent positions — you can roughly think of it as being tenured on arrival: as long as the university is not in financial crisis, the job is permanent and relatively stable. If you can land an R&T Lectureship at a UK Russell Group university or a top school in your field, it is absolutely worth considering. The UK also has research-only positions, from research assistant and postdoctoral researcher up to senior researcher — and at some universities, professorial research fellows. Research-only contracts are not permanent, but they are promotable just like the other two tracks. I am not sure whether senior researchers in the US can convert directly to tenured professorships, but in the UK there certainly are cases of senior researchers becoming professors. Overall, the UK system is more inclusive, and in some respects more humane.

The best part of a UK academic job is the absence of tenure-track pressure. The assessment pressure is comparatively low, and you can start doing research you actually find interesting from day one. New staff usually have a probation period of six months to at most four years (depending on the university), but I have almost never heard of anyone failing it. At Glasgow, assessment is done within your own discipline, benchmarked against the other 23 Russell Group universities. There are seven main criteria: publications, research funding, PhD supervision, teaching, leadership, societal impact, and esteem within the field. For publications the emphasis is squarely on quality — neither journal tiers nor impact factors count; it all comes down to peer review in the national research assessment (a complete enigma). Research funding is fiercely competitive everywhere these days, but there are plenty of doors to knock on: UKRI, the EU’s Horizon Europe, the European Space Agency, UK and global charities such as Wellcome, Leverhulme, and the Royal Society, and even industry and government contracts. The teaching load is generally not heavy — around two courses for new staff. And the UK evaluation system’s strong emphasis on societal impact has, in a sense, brought academic research closer to society.

Of course, UK academia has its downsides. First, early-career salaries are considerably lower than at US R1/R2 universities or in Hong Kong and Singapore, and hold no real advantage over Canada, Australia, or New Zealand. Since the 2008 financial crisis Europe’s growth has essentially stagnated, and the annual UK-wide university pay negotiations consistently fail to keep up with inflation (classic European egalitarianism); losing 1–2% a year compounds to roughly 20–30% underpayment over a decade or more — to say nothing of Europe’s generally high-tax welfare systems and the depreciation of the pound. The relatively better-paying UK institutions are mostly in London — Imperial, LSE, London Business School — but across Europe everyone is much of a muchness, with Switzerland the one high-salary, low-tax exception. Second, new staff get essentially no start-up package — a token amount at best — so the start is slower. The logic is understandable: you are hired into a permanent job, so unlike a tenure-track system that wants a clear return on a new hire, the up-front investment is smaller. Third, the PhD system: British PhDs are short, with funding typically only 3–4 years (UK government 3–3.5 years; CSC 4 years), which leaves little room for error — if the first year or two go badly you can extend by six months to a year, but once the funding runs out, finances get difficult. And because the PhD is short, graduates generally need a postdoc or a postdoctoral fellowship to accumulate a track record — it is hard to land a faculty job straight out of a PhD. At most 30% of UK government PhD studentships can go to international students, yet domestic students are not particularly keen on doing PhDs — so competition for the international quota is ferocious while the domestic 70% goes unfilled. In our centre’s urban big data area, I cannot recall recruiting a single British PhD student in the past five years. Fourth, research funding: the UK strongly emphasises interdisciplinarity — most medium-to-large grants demand cross-disciplinary collaboration and proactive network-building — and it is comparatively hard to find funding purely dedicated to basic research like the US NSF’s. Finally, UK universities have lately fallen into financial crisis as government restrictions on master’s students’ dependant visas cut international enrolment, prompting belt-tightening and job cuts everywhere — though thanks to the unions, most universities have relied on voluntary severance schemes or hiring freezes.

The empire’s sun set long ago, but its cultural influence is everywhere: UK academics routinely move to other Commonwealth countries, Hong Kong and Singapore, and the US (US faculty jobs generally require a US PhD or one from a top European school, otherwise a postdoc or prior US experience — though stars can go wherever they like). Because of Brexit, people can no longer move freely between the continent and Britain, but the Global Talent Visa is very friendly to researchers, especially postdocs — permanent residency after three years, and UK permanent residency carries none of the tax and residence constraints of a US green card. Housing is not cheap relative to income, but in general you can enjoy a rather good urban life.

Now for a few reflections on my own past five years, and the road ahead:

  • Our MSc in Urban Analytics grew from nothing, with scholarships every year to support Scottish students, and an exchange programme with Aalto University in Finland now in its second year. We will keep expanding links with continental Europe, hopefully towards more joint master’s and doctoral training.
  • I did a good deal of research on fundamental urban data — its production, processing, distribution, and extension. It has really sunk in that data will remain the core asset: LLM code and weights can be open-sourced, but genuinely open training data is rare.
  • I built up an urban sensing direction at the centre (UBDC Urban Sensing) with a miscellany of sensing projects. Going forward this will connect to urban sustainability and low-carbon/net-zero work, mainly in transport (air quality in Low Emission Zones) and housing (building energy efficiency models from local sensing).
  • The most fun of these was our sensor-enhanced housing survey — buying sensors and 3D-printing enclosures was a delight. The interdisciplinary multilingual cities project on new geographies of language was fascinating too.
  • During the pandemic I ran several short rapid-response projects, mostly analysing mobile phone app data, which taught me a great deal about delivering projects.
  • With friends and colleagues I organised Google Summer of Code for three years, building up a solid open-source Python package for spatial optimisation (PySAL/spopt), mainly the facility location modelling part. More updates to come.
  • I learnt to work with the UK media: our 2023 sensor project was covered by the Mirror (“UK facing summer health crisis as half of UK homes hit ‘sauna-like’ temperatures”) and many other outlets, and the final research report informed policy at London’s Southwark Council. In 2024 our Glasgow Low Emission Zone paper led to a BBC Radio Scotland interview and plenty of local coverage. I felt the power of the media — and became more careful about how information gets conveyed.
  • I helped organise conferences big and small: GIScience 2023, CPGIS 2023, GISRUK 2023, AGILE 2024, the Turing Urban Analytics Event 2024, Data-driven Decarbonisation in Scotland, and more. Do come visit Glasgow — even if our weather really is nothing to write home about.
  • The Gates Foundation-funded work on informal settlements in Nigeria and Kenya opened up our engagement with Africa (the IDEAMAPS Network).
  • My first PhD student, Niantang, graduated smoothly — all the best for the future!

For me personally, five years is neither long nor short — and three of them were pandemic years. I have come to believe the most important qualities for research are intrinsic interest and resilience/discipline: rejections of papers, and especially of grant proposals, are simply routine, and without genuine interest and toughness it is hard to keep going. Being a bit smarter on top of that is a bonus. Time management matters enormously — watch fewer short videos. In the next 3–5 years I hope to keep doing more interesting research and to further strengthen our undergraduate, master’s, and doctoral training. My thanks to everyone who has helped me over these years, and to the postdocs, PhD students, and master’s students who work alongside me. One person’s strength is always limited; a group of people is another matter entirely.
entry.summary: What five years on a UK academic post taught me about European academia — the job system, its pros and cons, and what we built at Glasgow.

entry.title: 2022 Year in Review: The New Normal

link.title: 2022 Year in Review: The New Normal

entry.content:
Translated from my original Chinese post on Zhihu (published December 2022). 中文原文请见知乎。

Today is the last working day of the year — tomorrow I set off for a holiday in Morocco! Hard to believe this is my first trip abroad since coming to the UK. Aside from being too lazy to sort out visas, the rest of the blame lies entirely with being stranded on this island by the pandemic.

I just re-read last year’s summary and found that the wishes basically all came true — except that I still have not made it to continental Europe. This year I attended five conferences within the UK: GISRUK 2022 (Liverpool), IMGS 2022 (Edinburgh), ABCP 2022 (Birmingham), RGS 2022 (Newcastle), and CATE 2022 (Edinburgh). IMGS and CATE both happened to be in Edinburgh — one upside of a small country is that joining the fun is easy. Counting students and postdocs, we gave six conference presentations in total, and I gave five invited academic talks myself. Next year I am involved in organising three major academic conferences: GISRUK 2023 in Glasgow, CPGIS 2023 in London, and GIScience 2023 in Leeds. If you have the time, the funding, and the visa, do come and have a look around the UK.

Publications came out slightly ahead of last year: 4 journal papers published, 4 under review, and 3 conference papers. There is still a backlog of papers sitting inside projects waiting to be revised and submitted — that will have to wait for next year. On the funding side it was a rather successful year: two ESRC-funded PhD studentship projects (one as PI, one as Co-I), a £300k SDAI grant (with a postdoc collaborator at the centre as PI), a £1.6m Gates Foundation project in Africa (Co-I), a 3D city modelling project for Glasgow City Council (PI), and two small teaching-focused grants supporting the Urban Analytics MSc (PI). Right at the end of the year an AGILE funding initiative grant (PI) landed as well, which lets us continue the spopt open-source development following this year’s GSoC project. Together with the existing UBDC projects, that adds up to quite a lot — the question now is how to turn it all into academic output and societal impact.

We advertised jobs all year long — three or four rounds of postdoc and faculty recruitment — so there will be plenty of new faces joining us next year. There are still 2 postdoc and 3 faculty positions open, plus a fully funded PhD studentship on African urban remote sensing (related to the IDEAMAPS theme) opening early next year.

On teaching: in spring 2022, with some international students still unable to come to the UK, I taught with pre-recorded videos plus a simultaneous in-person and Zoom livestream. By this autumn teaching had basically returned to pre-pandemic normality — it took almost three years to get back to normal.

Beyond my regular work at Glasgow, I took on some external roles this year: joining an ESRC grant assessment panel, serving as the final-year external examiner for Warwick’s Urban Analytics MSc, and sitting on the ESRC Future Data Services programme advisory board. The more I deal with funders, the more novel their internal workings seem — full of resource competition and negotiated compromise. The ESRC grant assessment panel was a particularly useful experience: you get to see how a research proposal passes through layer after layer of screening to the moment it finally wins funding. There are many uncontrollable factors along the way. My advice: polish your research ideas and your proposal properly, take every stage seriously (especially responses to reviewer comments), and after that — wish for luck. (One day when I have time I should write a piece specifically about the UK grant assessment process; it really is interesting.)

Finally, I should mention that I was promoted to Senior Lecturer (Associate Professor) this year. My thanks to the leadership and colleagues at Glasgow for their support and encouragement, and to the PhD students and postdocs I work with — onwards to the next goal.

On other fronts: I hope mainland China comes through this wave of the pandemic smoothly; once quarantine policies relax and flights recover next year, I plan to go back to China for a visit (I last left China in January 2016). The war in Ukraine shows no sign of stopping, European energy prices are sky-high, and with the Fed, the Bank of England, and the ECB raising rates relentlessly, a global recession is looming. I wish everyone good health and stable work — treasure the time with family and friends. Here’s to meeting more new friends next year, and to reunions with old ones!
entry.summary: Conferences returned, grants landed, teaching went back to normal, and a promotion to Senior Lecturer — a year-end summary.

entry.title: Some Thoughts on the UK PSW and HPI Visas

link.title: Some Thoughts on the UK PSW and HPI Visas

entry.content:
Translated from my original Chinese post on Zhihu (published June 2022). 中文原文请见知乎。

The UK recently opened applications for the new High Potential Individual (HPI) visa, which drew plenty of attention from UK-focused social media accounts and the press. The list of qualifying universities has been much discussed (and mocked). I initially thought the policy would not attract many people, but recently I saw several threads about this visa while browsing 1point3acres, so here are some quick thoughts.

The HPI visa is essentially an international version of the PSW visa. First, the PSW. The PSW visa was scrapped by Theresa May about a decade ago; it was roughly a British version of an unrestricted OPT: graduates of UK bachelor’s, master’s, or doctoral programmes could work legally for 2 years (3 years for PhDs) in any kind of job — at the extreme, you could spend two years waiting tables or running your own business. You do not need a job to apply — graduating is enough. It cannot be counted towards (five-year) settlement (the ten-year route should still count it), and it cannot be extended. If you want to stay and work beyond the 2–3 years, you need to switch to a regular work visa such as Tier 2 or the Global Talent Visa (the old Tier 1). In short, it is one of the UK’s post-Brexit measures to skim off talent.

The HPI extends the PSW idea to top universities worldwide: graduates from the past five years (in practice six years of rankings) of universities on a UK government list can apply. UK universities are not on the list (their graduates already have the PSW, whether the school ranks in the top ten or the hundreds). The list changes a bit across those five–six years; the universally recognised world top 30 are all on it, while schools ranked around 40–50 fluctuate. Because eligibility requires being in the top 50 of at least two of the three major world rankings (QS, THE, ARWU — I am not entirely sure), the list clearly favours universities in English-speaking countries (the US, Australia, Canada, and so on). Many excellent European universities are missing (roughly only Switzerland’s top two and a few in Germany, France, and Sweden make it — the absence of the Netherlands strikes me as absurd), and it is unfriendly to schools with top subject rankings but lower overall rankings. From Asia, the list includes mainland China’s top two, Singapore’s top two, HKU (HKUST some years), and the Universities of Tokyo and Kyoto. Like the PSW, the HPI itself cannot lead to settlement and cannot be extended — somewhat like the Dutch “search year” visa.

The visa homepage is here: High Potential Individual (HPI) visa. The rankings/eligibility are here: HPI visa eligibility.

Personally, I think the PSW and HPI are clearly good news for students who want to stay in the UK: more time to find a job, and the option to land in the UK directly from another country and start working. Many UK government jobs and smaller companies are reluctant to sponsor Tier 2; these visas provide a transition — two or three years to build UK work experience and try out life here, while looking for a sponsored route to settlement. In my own master’s programme this year, one student got a data-focused role with the Scottish Government before even graduating and used the PSW to take it up. He told me that without the PSW, the role simply could not have been offered to him.

The UK job market is of course much smaller. Finance is the mainstay; in IT, beyond branches of the US giants there are some smaller firms, and data science jobs are plentiful. Manufacturing is limited — Rolls-Royce, Dyson, ARM, some carmakers (Aston Martin, Jaguar) — plus pharma (GSK, AstraZeneca). Universities also offer plenty of jobs, and people are beginning to use this visa to recruit short-term research and teaching staff from abroad.

For PhD holders the visa is not hugely attractive: if you are doing a postdoc, the Global Talent Visa or Tier 2 route works fine without the extra hassle. I think the PSW matters far more than the HPI: if you have already studied in the UK for a few years, staying another two is familiar and convenient. The HPI, by contrast, means arriving from another country — and mostly from the world’s top universities, whose graduates can certainly find good jobs at home. Without prior UK study or networks, coming over for a two-year stint of work and life is no small challenge; and many people only consider the UK once they already have a job offer and work-visa sponsorship. As for how effective the HPI proves, we can wait for next year’s official statistics to see how many people actually applied.

Since Brexit, students and migrants from Europe have plummeted. Attracting high-skilled talent is certainly a good move — but how to replace the basic labour force that used to come from Eastern and Southern Europe will be a long-term problem.
entry.summary: What the Graduate (PSW) route and the new High Potential Individual visa actually offer — and who they are really for.

entry.title: Notes on the UK Global Talent Visa

link.title: Notes on the UK Global Talent Visa

entry.content:
Translated from my original Chinese post on Zhihu (published March 2022). 中文原文请见知乎。

Recently, while helping our centre recruit a new postdoc, I had to look into UK work visas for researchers. After exchanging notes with the university’s HR, I realised that this visa — updated from the old Tier 1 — is a major boon for recruiting talent into UK academia. Details are on the UK government website: Apply for the Global Talent visa (I must say the UK government’s visa pages are done remarkably carefully and thoughtfully).

The visa involves two steps: first obtaining an endorsement — essentially certification that you are a talent — and second, the visa application process itself.

There are four endorsement routes: (1) holding a faculty appointment at associate professor level or above, or a senior research position at an institution; (2) holding one of a list of hard-to-win individual research fellowships; (3) holding a postdoctoral position funded by the UK government or another endorsed funder; and (4) peer review. Of the four, the third is comparatively the easiest: if the applicant has a PhD, the grant runs for more than 2 years in total, and the total award exceeds £30,000, you can apply for the endorsement directly.

With an endorsement via the first three routes, you can apply for UK permanent residency (settlement) after three years. Via the fourth route it may take 3 or 5 years. Overall this is extremely good news for people hoping to build a stable academic research career in the UK, especially in less “hot” fields. The policy exists in part because, post-Brexit, the UK wants to attract more high-skilled talent to replace those who used to come freely from the continent — the reinstated PSW visa (two years of post-study work rights for UK graduates) is a similar measure.

Compared with the ordinary Tier 2 work visa, the Global Talent Visa has many advantages. Tier 2 can only be issued for 3 or 5 years; the Global Talent Visa can be as short as one year or as long as five. Tier 2 requires proof of English, and sensitive subjects need ATAS clearance; the Global Talent Visa requires neither, so applications are processed faster. Global Talent Visa holders can also be self-employed or take on other work, as long as it relates to their field.

That was my quick explanation in plain terms. Below are some specifics in English for reference. Because the Global Talent Visa can count time spent on certain other visa types towards settlement, someone nearing three years on a Tier 2 visa who meets the Global Talent criteria can switch to a one-year Global Talent Visa and apply for settlement immediately, rather than waiting out the full five years. For now the visa does not look hard to obtain; whether the UK government will tighten the policy over time — gradually raising the requirements to control numbers — remains to be seen.

  • Academic and research appointments (fast-track) — endorsement for individuals who have accepted a job in which they will have responsibility for academic, research or innovation leadership, or are an emerging leader. This includes but is not limited to Professor, Associate Professor and Senior Group Leader appointments. The appointment must be at an approved UK higher education institution or research institute, and certain recruitment requirements must be met. This route is administered by the British Academy, the Royal Academy of Engineering, and the Royal Society.
  • Individual fellowships (fast-track) — endorsement for individuals who have been awarded an individual fellowship on the list approved by the British Academy, the Royal Academy of Engineering, and the Royal Society. The fellowship must be held currently or within the last 12 months.
  • Endorsed funders (fast-track) — endorsement for researchers and specialists, employed or hosted by a department, faculty or college, whose name or job title is specified in a successful grant application from an endorsed funder approved by UKRI.
  • Peer-reviewed application (standard) — endorsement for individuals who meet all eligibility criteria and demonstrate that they are a proven leader in their field (Exceptional Talent) or have the potential to become one (Exceptional Promise). Applications are assessed by fellows of the British Academy, the Royal Academy of Engineering, or the Royal Society.

The most utilised route is the Endorsed Funder route. Its eligibility criteria are:

  • You have a grant award provided by an Endorsed Funder as shown in the Immigration Rules;
  • Your grant award is worth £30,000 or more (note: this is the total amount, not per annum or a salary equivalent);
  • Your grant award has a minimum duration of 24 months, including the time left on the contract;
  • Your grant award names you as the applicant under directly incurred costs.
entry.summary: Why the Global Talent Visa is a big win for recruiting researchers to UK academia — endorsement routes, settlement timelines, and practical details.

entry.title: 2021 Year in Review: The Pandemic, Research Interests, and Life

link.title: 2021 Year in Review: The Pandemic, Research Interests, and Life

entry.content:
Translated from my original Chinese post on Zhihu (published January 2022). 中文原文请见知乎。

Just back from skiing at Glencoe this afternoon — a good moment to fill the hole I dug at the start of the holidays.

2021 was, on the whole, a bewildering year: the pandemic raged from start to finish. In the first two months, the UK’s situation was so severe that nurseries closed, so I looked after the little one by day and started recording lectures after ten at night. Teaching programming entirely online is astonishingly time-consuming — a 3-hour tutorial could come with over an hour of extra Q&A. In the second half of the year, hybrid teaching became a serious test of classroom equipment: I ended up speaking into the microphones of the classroom desktop and my laptop simultaneously, both logged into Zoom sharing screens, while students in the room ideally answered questions into a phone logged into Zoom as well, so that online students could hear them. Even now I do not know how next semester will be taught. From a teaching perspective, though, the pandemic has been a catalyst — especially for a slow-moving system like the UK’s. ASU pushed fully online teaching hard years ago, and in hindsight it really was ahead of the curve. Good lecture material is now everywhere online; designing better small-group, discussion-based teaching experiences will be what distinguishes the quality of university education in the future. University was never just about sitting through lectures and collecting a diploma — the mutual learning and conversation with teachers and classmates often brings the most unexpected rewards.

The pandemic was not entirely without upside, especially for those of us in work-from-home-able fields. Governments everywhere printed money, the UK included — and after handing money to people and showering it on companies, a little was left over for research. In November 2020 the centre won an ESRC “quick spending” grant (grandly titled World Class Research Infrastructure: £213m to upgrade the UK’s research infrastructure, with UBDC receiving a further award from UKRI-ESRC) — to be spent by the end of March 2021. So from November onwards we scrambled to advertise and recruit. The original proposal had planned a travel survey, which COVID made impossible, so we amended it and bought mobile phone app data for the Greater Glasgow area — and the whole project became a full year of mobile phone app research in 2021. The results were excellent. First, this is a genuinely hot and practically meaningful direction: the centre has collaborated with Glasgow City Council for five or six years, and when they heard we had bought this data, they funded us to do more than half a year of government work on top — using app data to understand movement patterns during the pandemic: home location estimation, building OD matrices, and computing place visitation patterns. Second, on the data side, our collaborations with the vendors (Huq and Tamoco) went well enough that the centre decided to purchase their data covering the past 3 and coming 2 years for the whole UK, to be shared under conditions with the community for non-profit academic research. Third, in a very short time we hired one excellent postdoc and extended another postdoc’s contract, laying foundations for the research ahead. Doing research with novel data really is a blue ocean. Academia used to lack data, or could not afford high-quality data; companies lack the time, resources, and expertise to do socially useful but unprofitable analysis. Buying corporate data with public research funds and distributing it to academia saves enormous amounts of one-to-one contract negotiation, simplifies the process, and raises efficiency all round.

News about the centre’s mobility research: How people move around cities matters.

Beyond the new-forms-of-data research at the centre, a colleague and I ran a project on indoor environmental quality (Building capacity for sensor-enhanced surveys). It is a small project, but because it involves not only the social survey component but also a lot of hardware and software sensor integration, it has been great fun. Beyond that, I remain deeply interested in high-resolution thermal remote sensing, 3D cities, spatial location optimisation, and applied machine learning. In the summer, together with good friends at ASU, I organised a round of Google Summer of Code — a Brazilian student helped us write some fundamental spatial location optimisation algorithms into PySAL, with the paper under review at JOSS. I hope we can run another round next summer and further improve the reproducibility and openness of spatial optimisation methods: A summer of Python coding — open-source facility location modelling (spopt) development.

This year I joined the editorial board of Springer’s Urban Book Series. One perk is a free copy of every new book in the series. Urban science is so broad a field that I have yet to receive a book I could really sink into — or perhaps the discipline still trusts journals more, and book chapters always feel a touch less rigorous. In the second half of the year I joined the committee of the RGS-IBG Quantitative Methods Research Group, hoping to keep advancing quantitative methods in geography and urban science. Having served three years with the AAG’s Spatial Analysis and Modeling (SAM) group, I hope to foster some UK–US collaborative research next year.

Before year’s end, the centre won its next 2.5-year extension (Urban Big Data Centre’s data service awarded new funding from ESRC) — £4 million in total. From January 2022 the centre is hiring: two data scientists, one postdoc in urban remote sensing and sensing (the ad goes out in January — this one is mine, enquiries welcome), and a part-time project officer. Beyond those, a new PhD student will be recruited around March, working with the Salvation Army to analyse their Employment Plus programme, and four or five proposals are still awaiting final outcomes — if additional PhD or postdoc positions come through, more ads will follow.

Finally, papers. This year I published 4 journal articles, 1 book chapter, and 1 GIScience conference paper. The book chapter and one journal paper came out of first-year MSc student projects; the conference paper was a quick synthesis of this year’s phone app data research; two more journal papers were collaborations on optimisation and urban heat islands. My only first-authored piece wrapped up research from my 2018 postdoc days — finishing what I started. Citations saw no particular breakthrough — steady and unspectacular as ever. My pursuit in research remains the combination of long-term intellectual interest (fascinating, though often without direct use) and real societal value (useful — especially in today’s social sciences); who knows when a hot topic might land on your head. All of this, of course, rests on the academic freedom afforded by the UK’s permanent contracts from assistant-professor level onwards — chasing tenure would be a different game entirely. Three or four papers are under review, plus two deep-learning application papers with last year’s MSc students, three or four mobile-app and housing-sensor papers with the centre’s postdocs, and a queue of proposals that naturally need writing — next year will be full of writing tasks.

New Year’s wish: may the pandemic dissipate, so that in 2022 I can attend an in-person conference and finally visit continental Europe. Thanks to all colleagues, friends, teachers, and collaborators for their help and support, and to my family for their constant company. Since I came to the UK, three of my relatives have passed away in the past two and a half years, and in 2019 I also lost a close childhood friend; I myself have not been back to China for six years. So my one wish is that everyone stays healthy, safe, and happy — until we meet again.
entry.summary: Teaching through lockdowns, mobile phone app data as a research blue ocean, the first Google Summer of Code, and hopes for reunions.

entry.title: Urban, Geographic and Social Data Science at the University of Glasgow (updated May 2024)

link.title: Urban, Geographic and Social Data Science at the University of Glasgow (updated May 2024)

entry.content:
Translated from my original Chinese post on Zhihu (first published July 2021; last updated May 2024). 中文原文请见知乎。

Since 2019, the University of Glasgow has invested substantial dedicated funding to develop Data Science and AI across the whole university. Beyond hiring in the traditional data science and AI disciplines — computer science and statistics — the university has also invested heavily in urban/social science and geographic information science, to drive interdisciplinary integration between these fields, data science, and AI.

Across Geography and Urban Studies, Glasgow now has a combined urban/geographic-information data science and GIS/RS community of roughly 15–20 people, with more related positions (faculty, postdoc, and PhD) expected over the next 1–2 years.

Urban Studies & the Urban Big Data Centre

Phase one (2014–19):

  • Prof. Nick Bailey (University of Cambridge, UK) — housing and neighbourhoods, administrative big data
  • Dr Jinhyun Hong, Senior Lecturer (PhD: Urban Planning, University of Washington, USA) — 2022 update: Jin has moved back to the University of Seoul in South Korea but remains a UBDC affiliate.
  • Dr Jing Yao, Senior Lecturer (PhD: Geography, Arizona State University, USA) — GIScience, spatial statistics, spatial optimization, location modeling, health geography, regional science, urban and regional planning
  • Dr David McArthur, Reader (PhD: Business Economics, Norwegian School of Economics, Norway) — transportation, econometrics

Phase two (2019–2024):

  • Dr Qunshan Zhao, Senior Lecturer (PhD: Geography, Arizona State University, USA) — GIScience, remote sensing, spatial analysis, location modeling and analysis, urban analytics, urban data science
  • Prof. Joao Porto de Albuquerque (PhD: Computer Science, Unicamp, Brazil & TU Dortmund, Germany) — urban analytics, sustainable development, GIS, information systems, urban/disaster resilience
  • Prof. Alison Heppenstall (PhD: GIS and AI, University of Leeds, UK) — geocomputation, machine learning, geospatial AI, agent-based modeling, smart cities
  • Dr Mark Livingston, Senior Lecturer (PhD: Public Health, University of Glasgow) — housing analytics, private rented sector — 2024 update: Mark retired in April 2024.
  • Dr Fiona Crawford, Lecturer (PhD: Transportation, University of Leeds) — travel behaviour, transportation planning
  • Dr Michael Sinclair, Lecturer (PhD: Natural Resources & Environmental Management, University of Haifa) — human–nature interaction, new forms of data
  • Dr Jose Rafael Verduzco-Torres, Lecturer (PhD: Urban Studies, University of Glasgow) — public transport accessibility, land economy and policy

Geospatial Data Science

  • Prof. Ana Basiri (PhD: GIS, Geodesy, and Geomatics, K.N. Toosi University of Technology, Iran) — geospatial data science, navigation, GPS
  • Dr Mingshu Wang, Senior Lecturer (PhD: Geography, University of Georgia, USA) — urban informatics, urban science, GIScience, geodata science, city and regional development
  • Dr Ziqi Li, Lecturer (PhD: Geography, Arizona State University, USA) — 2023 update: Ziqi has moved to Florida State University but remains affiliated with the University.
  • Dr Meiliu Wu, Lecturer (PhD: Geography, University of Wisconsin–Madison, USA) — 2024 update: Meiliu joined Glasgow in July 2024.
  • Dr Brian Barrett, Reader (PhD: GIS and Remote Sensing, University College Cork, Ireland) — remote sensing, terrestrial environment

Health Geography

  • Prof. Rich Mitchell — health inequalities, health geography
  • Prof. Peter Craig — public health, health inequalities
  • Prof. Petra Meier — health inequalities, systems science
entry.summary: A who's-who of Glasgow's growing urban/geographic data science community — Urban Studies & UBDC, Geospatial Data Science, and Health Geography.

entry.title: Thoughts on Choosing a University Major after Gaokao

link.title: Thoughts on Choosing a University Major after Gaokao

entry.content:
Translated from my original Chinese post on Zhihu (published June 2021). 中文原文请见知乎。

Gaokao results are out and application season has begun; with a relative in the family taking the exam this year, I did a little research. It turns out that even after more than a decade, choosing a university programme is still a headache. Educational resources remain glaringly unequal across provinces and cities, and competition for the top universities has not eased one bit.

People now live longer and longer, and what matters most is the capacity for self-directed learning — adapting quickly to a changing society. The constraints of one’s undergraduate major matter less and less. Still, some things are learnt faster and more efficiently when young: mathematics, languages, programming, and the like. Choosing a major matters, but choosing one does not lock you in. If you have no particular passion for any field, it is worth picking a major from which switching tracks is relatively easy. In STEM, such majors typically build solid foundations in mathematics, physics, and computing; with those foundations, the range of future options stays wide. In my own experience, I have moved through four distinct but interconnected fields since undergraduate — remote sensing/photogrammetry, geographic information science, industrial engineering, and urban planning — and what they share is precisely mathematics, physics, and computing; the difference lies only in how you apply the techniques and what problems you solve. Majors, in a sense, exist for the administrative convenience of universities; companies running projects simply need people who can solve problems — and will hire them from any background.

It must be acknowledged that different majors — and the industries behind them — differ enormously in competitiveness. At home or abroad, medicine, law, and business pay well (China’s healthcare system is a special case, though I suspect things will converge eventually), and computing has minted countless fortunes in recent years, drawing enormous crowds. When choosing, consider whether you would rather be an ordinary person in a hot field, or a top person in a slightly less fashionable field (including the humanities and social sciences) that genuinely interests you — resources always tilt towards the top people, whatever the industry. It is also worth watching decadal technology forecasts: when a disruptive technology emerges in an industry, it delivers outsized premiums to that industry’s practitioners in short order.

Many posts debate whether the university matters more, or the major, or the city. I would only say: adults do not make choices — they make trade-offs. When your score falls short, the question becomes which part to sacrifice, given your needs and family circumstances — and this ties directly to your plans after the degree. If you intend to pursue graduate study abroad or at home, a top programme at a relatively good university carries decisive weight, and the city hardly matters — you will move anyway. The city’s influence is more about horizons and environment. These days every major city is competing to attract young workers; you do not have to study in a megacity.

From what I have observed in the US and the UK, overseas universities still favour applicants whose undergraduate degrees come from the former 985/211 — now “Double First-Class” — universities. It is a simple screening heuristic for wading through vast application piles, and a rough gauge of a Chinese student’s quality and ability. In that respect the gaokao system retains real efficiency and advantages. That said, foreign universities are not rigid: some of China’s newer universities are rising fast — ShanghaiTech, SUSTech, Shenzhen, Soochow — as are the Sino-foreign joint ventures such as UNNC, XJTLU, NYU Shanghai, and Duke Kunshan, and graduates of these schools do not necessarily lose out on brand.

The gaokao is one of life’s important choices, but it is emphatically only a beginning; many more chances to choose lie ahead. Around me, plenty of young professors at overseas universities, and plenty of leaders in business and government, did their undergraduate degrees at all manner of schools — not the 985s people imagine. In the end, who goes furthest depends on how high you aim, how long you persist, and how much you love what you do. The four or five undergraduate years are the freest, most energetic, most possibility-filled years of a life — I hope everyone leaves themselves beautiful memories worthy of their youth.

Finally — a small advertisement for Remote Sensing at Wuhan University? The programme has alumni all over the world, decent room to grow, is not too exhausting, is not especially hard to learn, and offers plenty of opportunities to change tracks. Worth considering?
entry.summary: Reflections on picking degrees after China's college entrance exam — transferable foundations, hot versus interesting fields, and why the choice is only a beginning.

entry.title: COVID-19 and the Outlook for Higher Education in the UK and US

link.title: COVID-19 and the Outlook for Higher Education in the UK and US

entry.content:
Translated from my original Chinese post on Zhihu (published April 2020). 中文原文请见知乎。

First of all, I hope everyone comes through this pandemic safe and healthy.

The sudden outbreak of 2020 caught the whole world off guard. From the initial outbreak in China, to Italy, Spain, the UK, France, Germany, the US, and then the world, it has been a once-in-a-century scene. The US stock market hit historic circuit breakers multiple times, global markets tumbled, and companies went bankrupt, laid off staff en masse, or furloughed workers — in some ways an even grimmer picture than the 2008 financial crisis.

In my own industry — higher education — the pandemic has likewise been a heavy shock. All campuses closed; teaching, research, and administration moved entirely online; all conference travel and fieldwork was suspended. This society-wide standstill has had a comparatively small effect on those of us in computational social science and data science, but the blow to experimental disciplines is enormous. Essentially every university froze new hiring (one can imagine how brutal next year’s faculty and even postdoc market will be); some have already imposed salary cuts and furloughs, with layoffs likely the unavoidable next step. Apart from the top research universities in the UK and US — which can attract enough domestic students and large research income — many universities depend heavily on international student fees. Because of the pandemic, most students next academic year will play it safe and choose to study closer to home, so enrolment in the UK and US will take a significant hit (especially in the UK, freshly through Brexit, from which many European students have already turned back to the continent).

First, the international students who enrolled in 2019–20. This cohort of one-year UK master’s students has been unlucky: two consecutive semesters of university strikes, then the pandemic, and they just missed the PSW visa reform. A one-year programme is short to begin with; three potentially disruptive events in the middle is a real blow (the strike impact depends on whether your course convener took part — students on my own Urban Analytics programme were relatively unaffected). With global economic activity stalled, consumption depressed, and central banks flooding the system to prop up economies, a recession in the short-to-medium term looks certain. When this cohort graduates in December and enters the job market, conditions will be harsh; most will likely return to their home countries to look for work, with chances of staying in the UK much diminished.

For those starting one-year UK master’s programmes in 2020–21: most UK universities currently plan to open as scheduled, but the first semester may well move online, with proper reopening once the situation clarifies in semester two. With many English tests cancelled, many universities have relaxed this year’s English requirements — good news for many students — and pre-sessional English courses have all moved online. In the US, visa complications mean many universities are debating cancelling or postponing the fall 2020 semester in favour of a spring 2021 start.

Since the economy may take one or two years to recover to pre-pandemic conditions, going to university now to upgrade your skills for the future job market is a sound choice. For those interested in doctoral research, now is also not a bad moment: by the time you graduate in three or four years, the recovery should be bringing jobs and faculty openings back (the faculty market in our field has actually been decent these past two or three years, fierce competition notwithstanding). Over the next academic year or two, universities will serve as a buffer — expect many people with work experience to apply and return to study.

From the perspective of my own field, urban analytics, the pandemic has exposed urban development problems on every front. Do we still need megacities? How do we manage the movement of people in an emergency? How do we run cities efficiently? How do we revive community vitality and economic activity after the pandemic? All of these questions demand urban big data science, statistical/optimisation/simulation methods, and geographic information science. Our Urban Big Data Centre in Glasgow has recently set up a dedicated research work package on the urban impacts of COVID-19 — continuously updated, and worth following.

To borrow a line from the Queen’s address: the pandemic will end, and friends will meet again.
entry.summary: How the pandemic hit universities — hiring freezes, international enrolment risk — and why it may still be a good time to study.

entry.title: Application Season: How PhD Admission and Training Differ between the US and the UK

link.title: Application Season: How PhD Admission and Training Differ between the US and the UK

entry.content:
Translated from my original Chinese post on Zhihu (published August 2019). 中文原文请见知乎。

It has now been exactly four months since I arrived in the UK. Over these months I have gradually come to understand the British academic system and how undergraduates, master’s students, and doctoral researchers are trained — and with the 2020–21 application season about to begin, here is a primer. There are no doubt plenty of similar articles online; this is my own understanding.

Let us start with doctoral training.

In the US, applicants generally hope for a fully funded offer, which comes in several forms. The first is the fellowship: funding that does not require the student to work for their stipend and tuition waiver. Examples include NSF and NASA doctoral fellowships, university-funded fellowships, and those from major private foundations. Such fellowships are open to international students only in small proportions and are fiercely competitive. American students who win an NSF or NASA fellowship can generally bring their own funding to study with a renowned advisor of their choice.

Most remaining funded offers come as teaching assistantships (TA), research assistantships (RA), or service positions (graduate service assistantships). TAs grade, run labs, and sometimes teach a few sessions (or the whole course…). RAs work on the advisor’s research projects, writing papers and building products. Service posts are more miscellaneous — I will not expand here. With a TA/RA, the university generally waives all tuition and pays a living stipend (US$1,500–2,000 a month, varying by school). TA money usually comes from departmental teaching income (mostly undergraduates and professional master’s programmes); it is always oversubscribed, with priority to PhD students already in the department. RA money depends on the advisor’s grants. Sometimes every professor has projects, all the PhD students become RAs, and TAs open up to the department’s academic master’s students; other times nobody has projects and everyone fights tooth and nail over a handful of TA slots… In the US, when professors apply for grants, most write in how many PhD students or postdocs the project needs — a professor with many grants can recruit many PhDs and postdocs and build a large group.

For doctoral students aiming at faculty careers, RAs are usually preferred, since you co-author papers with your advisor — and papers are the hard currency of the faculty market. That said, TA experience — especially independent teaching — strengthens a teaching record, and some people prefer TAs because they leave more time for one’s own doctoral research and skill-building.

In the UK, the picture is entirely different.

Britain and the EU have dedicated doctoral funding schemes — some applied for by supervisors, some by students — so research grants themselves mainly hire postdocs. But most of these doctoral funds are restricted to UK or EU students and are unfriendly to internationals. Add that the UK has no real TA tradition and RAs are scarce, and self-funding or bringing your own funding (your government’s scholarship — for China, the CSC) is the norm for international students. This is why most STEM students still prefer to apply to the US: the odds of a fully funded offer are better (and there are more universities). UK universities do, however, often advertise TA- or RA-type work as formal jobs that enrolled PhD students can apply for — so you may well earn a wage after starting, though tuition is not necessarily waived.

On applications: US doctoral applications generally do not require a complete research proposal — the first two years include coursework to build knowledge before settling on a topic. In the UK, the research proposal is crucial. The main reason is that most British doctoral students spend their 3–4 years working chiefly on their own research (being self- or externally funded), with the supervisor acting as guide and helper rather than employer — so a strong research idea matters enormously.

The UK also has a degree called the MRes, designed for students who want a PhD but do not yet meet admission requirements, or are unsure whether doctoral study suits them. The UK’s “1+3” doctoral pathway runs through the MRes as its first year: a series of required courses plus electives in your research area, building you up to doctoral research standard — much like the first year or two of a US PhD.

Personally, I think each system has its merits. A US PhD takes longer — typically 5–6 years straight from a bachelor’s, 3–4 with a master’s. A UK PhD straight from a bachelor’s can be done in 1+3; with a master’s, about 3 years. As for the advisor relationship: in the US your graduation timeline is heavily influenced by your advisor, sometimes beyond your control; in the UK it is rather better, since supervisors mostly guide rather than employ.

And finally, a little advertisement: if you are interested in doing a PhD with us in Urban Studies and the Urban Big Data Centre (UBDC) at Glasgow, start by reading the university’s PhD requirements and scholarship information — then send your CV, one or two representative pieces of work, and a research proposal to us at UBDC. Applications warmly welcome.
entry.summary: Fellowships, TAs and RAs versus research proposals and the MRes — what four months in the UK taught me about the two doctoral systems.
